Name: infotheoreticdistortionbasedcompression
Title: Information-Theoretic Distortion-Based Model Compression for Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating model compression as a rate-distortion optimization problem, we can achieve a better trade-off between model size and performance. Specifically, we aim to derive a theoretically grounded method for quantization and pruning that minimizes task-relevant distortion while adhering to a specified bit budget.
Related Work: Recent works such as 'Population Risk Improvement with Model Compression' (Bu et al., 2021) and 'Rate-Distortion Theoretic Model Compression: Successive Refinement for Pruning' (Isik et al., 2021) have explored the information-theoretic foundations of model compression. However, these works primarily focus on theoretical bounds and do not provide practical, end-to-end compression methods. Our proposal aims to bridge this gap by developing a practical framework that leverages rate-distortion optimization to achieve state-of-the-art compression while maintaining or improving model performance.
Abstract: We propose a novel information-theoretic framework for model compression in deep neural networks. By formulating compression as a rate-distortion optimization problem, we aim to minimize task-relevant distortion while adhering to a specified bit budget. Our method, Information-Theoretic Distortion-Based Compression (IT-DBC), integrates quantization and pruning into a unified framework. We derive theoretical bounds on the achievable compression rates and propose practical algorithms to achieve these bounds. Through experiments on various deep learning models and datasets, we demonstrate that IT-DBC achieves better trade-offs between model size and performance compared to existing methods.
Experiments: Derive theoretical bounds on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off for different types of deep neural networks (e.g., CNNs, Transformers).
Implement the IT-DBC framework for quantization and pruning, using mutual information and entropy as distortion measures.

Conduct experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., ImageNet for image classification, SQuAD for question answering) to evaluate the performance of IT-DBC in terms of model size, inference time, and accuracy.
Compare IT-DBC with state-of-the-art compression methods (e.g., Hessian-weighted K-means clustering, AMP-ViT, SNIP) using metrics such as compression ratio, accuracy, and inference latency.
Analyze the impact of different distortion measures (e.g., mutual information, cross-entropy) on the compression performance and model accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds derived may be loose in practice, leading to suboptimal compression performance.
The computational complexity of estimating mutual information and entropy may be high, especially for large models.
The proposed framework may not generalize well to all types of deep neural networks, particularly those with highly complex architectures.
There may be hardware-specific limitations that affect the practical implementation of the IT-DBC framework.
